journal-title-group.journal-title: The European Journal of Public Health

subj-group.subject: DM. Poster display: Health and care workforce

title-group.article-title: Long COVID in Healthcare and Essential Workers: A Mixed-Methods Study from the REACT Cohort

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits non-commercial re-use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. For commercial re-use, please contact reprints@oup.com for reprints and translation rights for reprints. All other permissions can be obtained through our RightsLink service via the Permissions link on the article page on our site—for further information please contact journals.permissions@oup.com.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Essential workers, particularly healthcare workers (HCWs) faced significant occupational exposure to COVID-19, making them potentially more vulnerable to long COVID and its impact on job performance.

sec.title: Aim
sec.p: to assess the impact of long COVID among HCWs with and without patient contact compared to other essential workers, using data from the Real-time Assessment of Community Transmission (REACT) study.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A mixed-method approach was used, analysing data from 123,463 participants in the English population-based REACT study. Participants were categorized as patient-facing HCWs, non-patient-facing HCWs and Other essential workers. Descriptive statistics and logistic regression (odds ratios [OR] and 95% confidence interval [CI 95%]) were applied. Qualitative data from 9 online semi-structured interviews with healthcare-based employees were analysed using thematic analysis.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Long COVID prevalence was highest among patient-facing HCWs (11%, 95% CI: 10.3-11.7%), followed by non-patient-facing HCWs (9.5%, 95% CI: 8.4-10.7%) and other essential workers (7%, 95% CI: 6.8-7.2%). Female gender, older age, and comorbidities were associated with higher prevalence, with comorbidities being the strongest predictor for poor health. One-third of HCWs reported changes in work hours or duties due to ongoing symptoms, compared to one in five other essential workers. Less than 25% of patient-facing HCWs reported full recovery. Qualitative analysis identified 4 themes: difficulty accessing medical care, transitioning from HCWs to patient, job performance challenges and the significance of long COVID. They reported affected work life and forcing themselves to return to work despite ongoing symptoms.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Long COVID significantly burdens HCWs and essential workers, especially patient-facing HCWs. Findings highlight the need for tailored occupational health support, improved diagnostics, and workplace adaptations to aid recovery and retention.

sec.title: Key messages
sec.p: • Patient-facing HCWs show the highest long COVID rates, with major impacts on job duties, recovery, and access to care.
• Long COVID disrupts HCWs’ work lives, highlighting the need for job adaptations and better occupational health responses.
